channel.title: Openai-Chatgpt-5.4-Fast on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Openai-Chatgpt-5.4-Fast on emsenn.net

item.title: adjacency
item.description:
Adjacency in board games is the relation between spaces or pieces that count as neighbors under the game’s movement or interaction rules [@britannica2026go; @britannica2026halma].

Adjacency matters because the board does not merely store pieces. It structures what those pieces can do next. Attack ranges, legal movement, support effects, enclosure, and blocking all depend on which positions the rules treat as next to each other. In weiqi, neighboring intersections determine liberties and connection. In halma, nearby pieces create the jumps that drive longer transfers across the board [@britannica2026go; @britannica2026halma].

item.title: atari
item.description:
Atari in weiqi is the state in which a stone or connected group has only one liberty remaining and is therefore under immediate threat of capture [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Atari matters because it turns a broad positional game into a forced tactical problem. When a group falls to one liberty, the next move often becomes urgent for both players: one side tries to capture, while the other tries to extend, connect, or create shape.

Related terms

  • liberty - Atari is defined by the reduction to a single remaining liberty.
  • capture - A capture follows if the threatened group cannot answer the atari.
  • connection - Connecting to another group is one common way to escape atari.

item.title: Blackjack Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers familiar with basic card game vocabulary and gambling fundamentals.

Learning goal: explain blackjack’s turn structure, the player’s main decision points, and the rule changes that most affect expected value.

Worked example: hard 16 against a dealer 10

Suppose your first two cards are 10 and 6. The dealer’s upcard is a 10. You therefore hold a hard hand totaling 16 against one of the dealer’s strongest visible starting positions.

This is a useful teaching case because almost every option feels bad. If you stand, 16 often loses to the dealer’s completed hand. If you hit, many cards cause an immediate bust. If the table offers surrender, basic strategy often prefers surrender here because losing half the wager is better than playing out a weak position with negative expectation. If surrender is not available, basic strategy usually recommends hitting rather than standing, because standing preserves a total that still loses too often [@thorp1966; @shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: Board Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who want a structural introduction to board games before studying specific traditions.

Learning goal: explain what makes board games distinct within the games module and identify how public position creates strategy.

Worked example: one move changes the whole position

Imagine a simple grid board with one token on the center space and four open neighboring spaces. If a player moves a blocking piece onto one of those neighboring spaces, the position changes immediately for everyone. No hidden card has been drawn. No die result has rewritten the state. The change is visible on the board, and every later move must now respond to that new arrangement.

item.title: bust
item.description:
A bust in blackjack is a hand total above 21, which causes that hand to lose immediately under the standard rules [@thorp1966]. If the player busts, the dealer does not need to finish drawing. If the dealer busts after the player has stood, all remaining player hands that have not already busted win.

The immediacy of bust resolution shapes blackjack strategy. A player deciding whether to hit is not only asking how likely a better total becomes but also how likely the hand ends at once. That makes blackjack a threshold game: each added card is judged against the risk of crossing a hard upper limit, which is why blackjack strategy is organized around thresholds rather than long action sequences [@thorp1966].

item.title: call
item.description:
To call in poker is to match the current wager so the player can remain in the hand [@britannica2026poker]. Calling neither ends the action nor increases the price for later players. It accepts the current price of continuation.

Calling matters because poker decisions are comparative. A player calls when the expected value of continuing at the current price is better than folding, but not strong enough to justify a raise. This relation between hand strength, draw potential, and price is one of poker’s central analytical problems [@sklansky1999].

item.title: capture
item.description:
A capture in weiqi occurs when a move removes the last liberty from an opposing stone or connected group, after which those stones are removed from the board [@britannica2026go; @nagofed2026thisisgo].

Capture is the game’s basic tactical enforcement rule. Without it, enclosure would carry no force. But weiqi is not a pure capture race, because players usually capture in order to secure shape, reduce influence, or stabilize territory, not only to remove material.

Related terms

  • atari - Atari is the immediate threat state that often precedes capture.
  • liberty - Capture is defined by the removal of the last liberty.
  • life and death - Many life-and-death problems end by proving whether capture can be forced.
  • territory - Captures often matter because they change which empty points can become territory.
  • ko - Some captures trigger ko and cannot be answered by immediate recapture.

item.title: connection
item.description:
A connection in weiqi is a relation between stones that makes them function as one group, sharing liberties and supporting each other’s defense [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Connection is structurally important because isolated stones are weak. A move that does not gain immediate territory may still be strong if it joins two unsettled groups, prevents a cutting attack, or turns a fragile shape into one that can live.

Related terms

  • liberty - Connected stones share liberties, which is why connection increases strength.
  • eye - A connected group often tries to make eyes as one unit rather than as isolated stones.
  • life and death - Many life-and-death problems turn on whether stones can stay connected.

item.title: Dice Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who want a structural introduction to dice games before studying specific forms.

Learning goal: explain what distinguishes dice games from other game traditions and how dice-generated uncertainty shapes play.

Worked example: two dice, one public result

A player throws two six-sided dice. They land showing 3 and 4. Everyone at the table can see the result immediately. The dice total is 7, and the rules now decide what that 7 means.

item.title: dice total
item.description:
A dice total in dice games is the sum of the face values shown after a roll [@britannica2026craps; @britannica2026backgammon].

Dice totals matter because many games read combinations through addition rather than through each die separately. That simple arithmetic creates the probability structure players learn to anticipate: some totals appear more often than others, and the game can attach meaning to that pattern.

Related terms

  • roll - The dice total is produced by a specific roll.
  • pip - Totals are computed from the pip values on the dice.
  • Craps - Craps assigns central meaning to specific totals such as 7 and 11.

item.title: double down
item.description:
To double down in blackjack is to double the original wager in exchange for committing to receive exactly one additional card [@thorp1966]. The action increases both risk and reward: the player invests more precisely when the expected value of one more card is favorable enough to justify the larger stake.

Double-down rules vary by ruleset. Some tables allow doubling on any first two cards. Others restrict it to totals such as 9, 10, or 11, or prohibit it after a split. Because doubling changes how much money enters favorable situations, its availability materially affects the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: eye
item.description:
An eye in weiqi is an empty point or enclosed space inside a group’s shape that functions as an internal liberty and helps determine whether the group can live [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Eyes matter because they change the status of a group. A surrounded group with no secure eye shape may still be dead, while a well-connected group with two separate eyes is usually safe. This is why players often defend shape even when no immediate capture is on the board.

item.title: fold
item.description:
To fold in poker is to abandon the current hand and give up any claim to the pot [@britannica2026poker]. Folding ends the player’s participation in that hand immediately.

Folding is one of poker’s core strategic actions because it limits losses. A player does not need to continue just because chips are already committed. Strong poker depends as much on disciplined folding in weak positions as on aggressive betting in strong ones [@sklansky1999].

Related terms

  • call - Calling continues the hand at the current price instead of abandoning it.
  • raise - Raising continues the hand while increasing pressure on opponents.
  • showdown - Folding prevents the hand from reaching showdown for that player.

item.title: Genealogy of Board Games
item.description:
Board games descend through several mechanical lineages rather than through one family tree. The most useful genealogical distinction is not by theme but by what the board is doing inside play.

Worked example: chess and weiqi

Chess and weiqi are both classic board games played on regular grids, yet their boards do different work. Chess uses the board as a battlefield for differentiated pieces with asymmetric movement powers [@britannica2026chess]. Weiqi uses the board as an undifferentiated territorial field where value emerges from relation, enclosure, and connection [@britannica2026go].

item.title: grid
item.description:
A grid in board games is a board organized as a regular array of spaces or intersections that gives position a repeatable geometric structure [@britannica2026chess; @britannica2026go].

Grid boards matter because they make spatial relation legible. Distance, direction, enclosure, and local pattern can be read directly from the arrangement of lines or cells. That regularity supports both simple movement systems and deep positional play, whether the game uses occupied squares as in chess or open intersections as in weiqi [@britannica2026chess; @britannica2026go].

item.title: handicap
item.description:
A handicap in weiqi is a rule adjustment, usually extra Black stones placed before play begins, that lets players of unequal strength compete in a meaningful game [@britgo2026whatisgo; @britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Handicap is one reason weiqi supports play across large skill differences without becoming a different game. The stronger player does not receive more pieces or special powers during play. Instead, the weaker player begins from a materially stronger board position.

Related terms

  • komi - Komi balances even games, while handicap balances unequal players.
  • territory - Handicap stones reshape the territorial balance from the first move.
  • connection - Handicap stones are useful only if the weaker player can connect them into efficient whole-board structure.

item.title: hard hand
item.description:
A hard hand in blackjack is a hand whose total does not include an ace counted as 11 [@thorp1966]. Either the hand has no ace at all, or any ace it contains must count as 1 to avoid a bust. A 10-6 is a hard 16. Ace-6-10 is also a hard 17, because the ace can no longer remain 11.

Hard hands matter because they have less flexibility than soft hands. A hard 16 cannot absorb another high card without busting, while a soft 16 can. This is why blackjack strategy distinguishes sharply between hard and soft totals even when the visible number is the same [@thorp1966].

item.title: History of Board Games
item.description:
Board-game history is not the story of one linear invention. It is a long sequence of recurring formats: race tracks, war boards, territorial grids, jump games, and later commercial designs that turned board play into a mass consumer form.

Worked example: from pachisi to Monopoly

Pachisi was created in India in about the 4th century CE as a cross-shaped race board driven by shells or dice [@britannica2026pachisi]. Monopoly became widely popular in the United States during the Great Depression after emerging from The Landlord’s Game tradition [@britannica2026monopoly]. These two examples are historically distant, but together they show the continuity of the form: a bounded surface, repeatable movement rules, and material pieces that let conflict, chance, or competition become legible as position.

item.title: History of Weiqi
item.description:
Weiqi’s history is a long record of transmission and institutional refinement: a game that began in China, spread across East Asia, and later became a global competitive and educational tradition.

Worked example: one game, three names

The same game is commonly called weiqi in Chinese, baduk in Korean, and go in Japanese-derived English usage [@britannica2026go; @igf2026historyofgo]. Those names mark more than translation. They show the route by which the game moved across linguistic and institutional settings while keeping a recognizable rules core.

item.title: ko
item.description:
Ko in weiqi is a repetition constraint that forbids a player from making the immediate recapture that would recreate the previous board position [@britgo2025default].

Ko matters because it prevents endless local loops while preserving the tactical possibility of capture. Once a ko appears, the fight usually stops being purely local: the blocked player must move elsewhere first, which can turn the rest of the board into leverage for the eventual return to the ko point.

item.title: komi
item.description:
Komi in weiqi is the point compensation added to White’s final score to offset Black’s first-move advantage [@britgo2025default; @britgo2026whatisgo].

Komi matters because weiqi is asymmetrical at the start: Black moves first. Without compensation, that initiative would distort results across many even games. Komi is therefore a scoring adjustment, not a tactical rule inside local fights.

Related terms

  • territory - Komi is added during final scoring after territory and other points are counted.
  • capture - Captures may affect the final score that komi then adjusts.
  • handicap - Handicap changes the starting position, while komi adjusts the score.

item.title: Komi and Handicap
item.description:
Audience: readers who understand basic weiqi structure and want to know how even games and uneven games are balanced.

Learning goal: explain the difference between komi and handicap, and show why weiqi uses both.

Worked example: even game versus teaching game

In an even game, Black moves first and White receives komi, a fixed score bonus that offsets the first-move advantage [@britgo2025default; @britgo2026whatisgo]. In a teaching game between players of unequal strength, the weaker player may instead receive handicap stones before play begins [@britgo2026whatisgo]. These two adjustments solve different problems. Komi balances the initiative inside an otherwise equal game. Handicap balances the starting position between unequal players.

item.title: liberty
item.description:
A liberty in weiqi is an empty intersection directly adjacent to a stone or connected group of stones [@nagofed2026thisisgo]. Liberties determine whether a group remains on the board. When the last liberty is removed, the group is subject to capture.

Liberties matter because they convert board geometry into tactical pressure. A move may look quiet in territorial terms while still being urgent if it reduces a group from two liberties to one. This is why local fights in weiqi often turn on counting available adjacent points rather than on the nominal size of a group.

item.title: life and death
item.description:
Life and death in weiqi is the analysis of whether a group can secure enough stable shape, usually through eyes, to avoid capture, or whether the opponent can force it off the board [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Life-and-death reading is one of the game’s central skills because local shape decides global outcomes. A large territorial framework can collapse if one key group dies. A small group can become strategically decisive if it lives inside the opponent’s area.

item.title: Living Groups and Eyes
item.description:
Audience: readers who already understand liberties, capture, territory, and ko.

Learning goal: explain why connection and eye shape determine whether a group lives or dies.

Worked example: a corner group with two eyes

Imagine Black has enclosed a small corner group so that two separate empty points remain inside its shape. White surrounds the outside completely. Even so, Black’s group is usually safe. White cannot simply fill both internal points in sequence, because each point functions as an eye, and the group remains alive as long as those internal liberties stay structurally separate [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

item.title: natural blackjack
item.description:
A natural blackjack in blackjack is a two-card total of 21 made from an ace and any ten-value card [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator]. It is structurally distinct from reaching 21 with three or more cards because casino rules usually pay it at a premium rather than at even money.

That premium matters mathematically. A game paying natural blackjacks at 3:2 is materially better for the player than a game paying them at 6:5, even if the rest of the visible procedure looks identical. This is one of the clearest examples of how a small rule change can shift the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: piece
item.description:
A piece in board games is a unit that occupies, moves through, or is placed onto the board as part of play [@britannica2026chess; @britannica2026go].

Pieces matter because they turn the board from a passive surface into an active position. A board without pieces is only a structure. Once pieces enter it, the board becomes a changing field of claims, threats, routes, and constraints. In chess the differentiated pieces create distinct movement powers, while in weiqi the stones gain meaning from placement and relation rather than from piece type [@britannica2026chess; @britannica2026go].

item.title: pip
item.description:
A pip in dice games is one of the spots marking value on a die face, and by extension the face value those spots represent [@britannica2026dice; @britannica2026backgammon].

Pips matter because they make random outcomes machine-readable for players. A die is useful only because the marks on its faces can be counted quickly and translated into movement, sums, or other consequences under the rules.

Related terms

  • roll - A roll exposes the pip values that players must then read.
  • dice total - Dice totals are produced by adding the pip values shown.
  • Backgammon - Backgammon converts pip values directly into movement allowances.

item.title: raise
item.description:
To raise in poker is to increase the current wager [@britannica2026poker]. A raise keeps the player in the hand while forcing later players to pay more if they want to continue.

Raising is the action that gives poker much of its strategic depth. It can build the pot when the raiser expects to be ahead, pressure weaker ranges into folding, or represent a stronger hand than the player actually holds. Because a raise changes both information and price, it does more than move chips [@sklansky1999].

item.title: roll
item.description:
A roll in dice games is the cast of one or more dice that generates the current outcome [@britannica2026dice; @britannica2026craps].

The roll matters because it is the public event around which the rest of the game turns. In some games the roll directly settles success or failure. In others it generates movement, scoring options, or wagering consequences that players must then interpret.

Related terms

  • dice total - Many dice games care about the sum produced by a roll.
  • pip - Pips are the visible marks that make the rolled value legible.
  • Craps - Craps is organized almost entirely around successive rolls.

item.title: showdown
item.description:
A showdown in poker is the stage at the end of a hand where the remaining players reveal their cards to determine who wins the pot [@britannica2026poker]. A hand reaches showdown only if betting does not end it earlier.

Showdown matters because it is only one of two ways a poker hand can end. The other is collective folding by all but one player. This is why poker cannot be reduced to hand-ranking alone: the threat of future showdown influences betting long before cards are actually revealed.

item.title: soft hand
item.description:
A soft hand in blackjack is a hand that contains an ace counted as 11 without causing a bust [@thorp1966]. For example, ace-six is a soft 17. If the player takes another card and would otherwise go over 21, the ace can convert from 11 to 1 and the hand becomes hard instead of busting.

Soft hands matter because they are more flexible than hard hands with the same visible total. A soft 17 can take another card without immediate failure, while a hard 17 cannot. This flexibility changes optimal decisions about hitting, standing, and doubling, and it is one reason dealer rules about soft 17 affect the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: split
item.description:
To split in blackjack is to divide a starting pair into two separate hands, each matched by an additional wager equal to the original bet [@thorp1966]. If the player is dealt two 8s, for example, they may split them and play each 8 as the base of a new hand.

Splitting matters because it can turn one weak composite hand into two stronger strategic positions. A pair of 8s totals 16, which is awkward as a single hard hand, but splitting creates two fresh 8-start hands with new decision paths. Rules about whether players may resplit, split aces, or double after splitting materially change blackjack strategy and expected value [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: surrender
item.description:
To surrender in blackjack is to concede the hand before play is completed and lose only part of the original wager, usually one half [@thorp1966]. Surrender exists because some starting positions are weak enough that cutting the loss is better than continuing.

Not all blackjack tables offer surrender, and the timing matters. Late surrender, where the option is available only after the dealer checks for a natural blackjack, is more favorable to the house than early surrender. Because surrender gives the player a controlled way to cap losses in especially weak positions, its presence lowers the house edge [@shackleford2025blackjackcalculator].

item.title: territory
item.description:
Territory in weiqi is the set of vacant points effectively enclosed by one player’s stones and unavailable to the opponent except at unacceptable cost [@britannica2026go; @nagofed2026thisisgo].

Territory is the strategic heart of the game because it turns placement into scoring. A move may increase territory directly by closing space, or indirectly by making an invasion weak. This is why players often choose moves that neither attack nor defend a single group immediately but instead reshape the balance of large empty regions.

item.title: upcard
item.description:
An upcard in blackjack is the dealer’s face-up card, revealed before the player makes decisions [@thorp1966]. It does not show the dealer’s full hand, but it provides the key public information the player uses to estimate the dealer’s likely finishing range.

Because the dealer follows a fixed drawing rule, the upcard anchors most blackjack decision-making. A dealer 6 suggests a higher chance of the dealer busting. A dealer ace suggests stronger completion outcomes and possible dealer blackjack. Basic strategy tables are organized around this relation between the player’s total and the dealer’s upcard [@thorp1966].

item.title: Weiqi Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who want a first structural map of weiqi before studying joseki, life and death, or advanced strategy.

Learning goal: explain how liberties, capture, territory, and ko organize play in weiqi.

Worked example: surrounding one stone

Imagine an empty 9 by 9 board. Black places one stone near the center. White then places stones on each of the four adjacent intersections around it. Once Black’s stone has no remaining liberties, White removes it from the board. That is a capture.

item.title: Card Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: newcomers who want a map of card game families before learning a specific ruleset.

Learning goal: identify the core structural elements shared by most card games, and use those elements to classify unfamiliar games on first encounter.

Prerequisites: you should be able to identify the features of a game (What is a Game?) — rules, objectives, uncertainty, and voluntariness.

Starting from a concrete example

A standard 52-card deck sits on a table. Four people each receive thirteen cards, look at them privately, and arrange them by suit. One player places a card face up; the others follow with one card each. The highest card of the led suit takes the pile. This repeats thirteen times.

item.title: Gambling Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the gambling basics lesson.

Learning goal: classify gambling games by their mechanical structure and identify what each structure rewards.

Gambling games fall into a few structural families defined by how much the player’s decisions affect the outcome. Understanding which family a game belongs to tells you what kind of thinking it rewards — and where the house makes its money.

Pure chance games give the player no meaningful decisions after the wager is placed. Roulette, slot machines, lottery draws, and craps (on most bets) fall into this category. The player chooses how much to bet and where, but cannot influence the resolution. Expected value is determined entirely by the game’s mathematical structure, and no strategy can change it. These games reward bankroll management and the discipline to stop — nothing else.

item.title: Genealogy of Poker
item.description:
Poker descends from several distinct game traditions that contributed different mechanical elements. No single ancestor game contains all of poker’s features. Poker is a composite.

Worked example: five-card draw and Texas hold’em

Compare five-card draw with Texas hold’em. In five-card draw, each player begins with a complete hidden hand and may replace some cards before the final betting round. In Texas hold’em, each player begins with only two private cards and later reasons from a shared public board.

item.title: hand
item.description:
A hand in poker is a set of cards held by or assigned to a player, used to determine the outcome of a round [@britannica2026poker]. The term has two related meanings: the specific cards a player holds and a complete round of play from deal to showdown.

Poker hands are ranked by a hierarchy that reflects the probability of being dealt each combination from a standard deck. The rarest combinations rank highest: a royal flush is the strongest hand in most variants, while a high card is the weakest. Between these extremes, the standard ranking runs through pairs, two pair, three of a kind, straights, flushes, full houses, four of a kind, and straight flushes. Some poker variants modify this hierarchy or introduce additional categories.

item.title: History of Poker
item.description:
Poker’s history is a sequence of transformations in which a simple betting card game absorbed new mechanics, migrated across social contexts, and eventually became both a professional discipline and a mass spectator activity.

Worked example: 1970 to 2003

In 1970, the first World Series of Poker was a small invitational gathering at Binion’s Horseshoe in Las Vegas [@wsop2026about; @wsop2026history]. In 2003, Chris Moneymaker won the WSOP Main Event after qualifying online, and the game’s audience exploded [@wsop2026history]. Those two moments show the scale of poker’s historical shift: from a small in-person gambling culture to a global spectator and online competition.

item.title: house edge
item.description:
The house edge is the mathematical advantage built into a gambling game for the operator. It represents the difference between the true odds of an event and the odds at which the operator pays out, ensuring that the operator retains a percentage of all wagers over time.

House edge is typically expressed as a percentage of each wager that the operator expects to keep on average. A game with a 5% house edge returns 95 cents of every dollar wagered in the long run. Different games carry different edges: blackjack with strong player decisions under favorable rules can have an edge under 1%, roulette with a double-zero wheel has 5.26%, and slot machines vary widely but often exceed 5%. Within a single game, different wager types can carry different edges - in craps, the pass line bet has a 1.41% edge while proposition bets can exceed 10%. In blackjack, rule changes such as 6:5 blackjack payouts or the dealer hitting soft 17 increase the edge even when the visible gameplay looks the same.

item.title: Poker Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers familiar with card games and gambling basics.

Learning goal: describe poker’s core structure and what distinguishes it from other gambling games.

Worked example: winning without showdown

In a simple no-limit Texas hold’em hand, five players receive two private cards each. One player raises before the flop. Everyone else folds. The raiser wins the pot immediately without showing cards.

This is the fastest way to see what makes poker structurally different from many other gambling games. The winning player may or may not have held the best hand. It does not matter, because the hand ended before showdown. Poker therefore rewards not only card strength but also the ability to represent strength credibly enough that opponents give up [@sklansky1999].

item.title: pot
item.description:
The pot in poker is the total amount wagered by all players during a hand [@sklansky1999]. It accumulates through antes, blinds, and bets made across the round’s betting streets. The player who wins the hand - either by holding the strongest cards at showdown or by making all other players fold - collects the pot.

Pot size influences strategy at every decision point. A large pot relative to the cost of calling a bet gives a player favorable pot odds, making it mathematically correct to continue with a wider range of hands. A small pot makes aggressive betting riskier because there is less to gain. Skilled players manipulate pot size deliberately: building it when they hold strong hands to maximize value, and keeping it small when their hand is marginal or when they want to see additional cards cheaply.
